item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this for a lot of reasons. It's an incorporeal entity that just wants to DANCE. The fact that the site has to get researchers in on the party to maintain containment of the anomaly is also fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like everything worked here except the note from the Ethics Committee in the containment procedures. Other than that, I very much enjoyed it, and the conga line procedure caught me off guard and made me audibly laugh. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated Safe->Euclid — thanks to all on this thread for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated Safe->Euclid — thanks to all on this thread for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The box" here is the city of Portland.

As far as the Foundation is concerned, "the box" are the nightclubs the SCP currently inhabits, as defined in the Procedures. It does not know that the SCP can't leave the city, and has already noted that the SCP can exponentially increase its numbers if left unchecked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, think of what happens should the Foundation walk away. SCP-3119 splits and splits and splits, but only at the rate that nightclubs fail (slower, since SCP-3119 is going to be a draw for dancers) i.e. once every 15 years or so. SCP-3119 can't travel large distances unaided and doesn't even want to, so the net result would be:

  • Portland gets an unusual number of nightclubs.

"The box" here is the city of Portland. I think I accept the downvotes :) thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Promising start for a new writer. I'm afraid I downvoted based mainly on the lapses in clinical tone. Use of "we" was surprisingly refreshing, and it's an innovation I'd like to see used elsewhere. Likewise with the generally didactic (as in the sense of "instructive"/"educational") manner - e.g. "which we define here as…" - which both aids the real-life reader's comprehension and, in-universe, fits canonically with the idea of these pages being introductory containment manuals. However, combined with passages like "music seems purer… radiate storge", these represent too large a break from the existing unwritten style guide. I tend to be quite conservative with this sort of thing, I'm afraid.

Speaking to the narrative: the entities are meant to be broadly sympathetic, and that's conveyed effectively, but I couldn't truly feel them. They're incorporeal emotionally as well as physically, somehow. The passage at the end with the "polite smile"/"blank stare" was a real highlight, and came closest to giving us a glimpse into their motivations; a little more description of their reactions like that would have helped bring them to life. I can't help but wonder if they'd have felt more real if they had some kind of actual projected avatar – if the person whose mind was being occupied experienced some distinct image or persona that they could recount to us in the document.

But I'm looking forward to seeing you write more here. You've got a distinctive approach to scip-writing that I want to see more of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing is, there's a guideline called the "locked box test". 9 times out if 10, it's an accurate indicator for what your object class should be.

The Locked Box Test is more or less as follows:

  • If you lock it in a box and it stays there, it's Safe.
  • If you lock it in a box and need to check up on every so often it to make sure it's still there, it's Euclid.
  • If you lock it in a box, and the only thing keeping it in the box is twine and prayer, it's Keter.
  • If it is the box, it's Thaumiel.

Strictly speaking, the Foundation is required to actively maintain two nightclubs and make sure they're popular venues, or the SCP breaches containment and they gotta set up or buy out more nightclubs. They can't forget about the SCP and assume it'll sit there indefinitely, they need to be actively working on its containment to make sure it stays put.

This is a solid Euclid, no two ways about it.

I want to tell an everyday story in the SCP universe, so that means I want 3119 to be Safe.

Then the choices here are to either scale back the effects of the SCP so it is no longer Euclid, or accept the fact that people are gonna say "this isn't Safe" and give downvotes as a result.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, let me think about that.

A big part of the feel I want to evoke is the work environment of the Foundation, here. If I had to make up the contained classification distributions I'd write down 90% Safe, 9% Euclid, 1% Keter, if nothing else because Safe objects are easier and cheaper to secure. I want to tell an everyday story in the SCP universe, so that means I want 3119 to be Safe.

You mention the 0% chance of 1470 breaching. Currently the containment procedures would supply 42 hours of club dancing plus say 4 of PROCEDURE-3119-AMERICAN-SMOOTH, with only 35 being needed. The 42h would be supplied organically by the nightclubs being open and staying popular even if the Foundation forgot about it…

The other route I could go down is that everyone knows this should be Euclid (as has been mentioned repeatedly) and the Site Director is refusing the reclassification for reasons of (workspace evoke, probably funding).

OK, I think I see a way forward, and I'll add it in an Addendum document linked next to the object classification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remember, danger or ill intent isn’t what causes classification. It’s difficulty of containment, as this does require active management to avoid ill effects its 100% Euclid. While it’s definitely not Keter, it’s closer to Keter than it is to being Safe. Re: the examples provided, 999 isn’t sapient and has little maintenance, while 1470 has 0% chance of ever breaching since it’s so easy to keep in a box.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, I had the same connection in mind when I read this.

I have the same concerns as Abettik, especially concerning the division process (it's rather specific despite being something that was only recorded once, and with invisible/incorporeal beings)… the trail they leave somewhat mitigates that, but I feel as though it could be phrased with more uncertainty. Also, going by the lockbox test, there's no way in hell this is Safe - it requires routine maintenance, without which it can become temporarily uncontainable, and hell, it interacts directly with the public. How benign something is doesn't really affect Object Class, or at least it's not as important as its capabilities.

Beyond that…this is far too insane to not love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for reading and the feedback!

  1. OK — changed several 'Foundation researchers' to 'The Foundation'. Thanks!
  2. I've defanged it a little bit even more by making it not want to go outside SCP-3119-C. I'm thinking of things like SCP-999 and SCP-1470 — it's never been violent, never hurt anyone accidentally, doesn't grow fast, doesn't move unless moved, they can interact with it directly, they think they have a way to kill it… SCP-3119 is quite a bit less dangerous than a big dog.

Added a link to Site-64 :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite well done for a first SCP, though there are a couple of stylistic issues.

  1. You frequently use plural pronouns to refer to the Foundation as a whole, that is non-clinical.
  2. This should really be Euclid. Even though they're low threat, they're still sapient entities that can propagate by division.

The actual plot arc was quite nice however, and the image of Conwell's menagerie of characters dancing the night away after a hard day's work is great. +1

Btw, any chance you can make the mention of Site-64 into a crosslink to the Site-64 Hub?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the feedback — I'll go grab lunch, then incorporate your collapsible.

+ show block
– hide block

You're not really being scientific or clinical with this document. For instance, how, exactly, do we know that these things are sapient? How do we even know that they are entities if there's no way to see/measure them? It sounds like you're taking the word of the people "possessed," in which case it could very well be memetic.

Researchers have direct experience of being infested by SCP-3119, as stated late on in the text. I'll clarify by adding a note earlier on, in the section you identified.

We only know of two instances, but you claim that during a "New Yorker" event, they will split in two. So I guess this only happened once, and we're assuming it happens every time? Then you claim the instances "accelerate" away from -C. Like how do we know where they're going, what they're doing if we can't see or track them? How do we even know where they went, or what they did?

This is a definite weakness in the text, and one I had consciously decided to let stand for reasons of length. SCP-3119 is very easy to detect and monitor using <whatever the standard telepathic methods are>. I don't really want to describe the standard methods, and referring to them added to length without serving an obvious purpose. Since you've called it out, I'll clarify in the text that this is the case.

Yeah, no, this really needs to be at least Euclid. Things far easier to contain are Euclid.

See above. It's well understood, friendly, and reliably contained.

What does that even mean? [snip]

SCP-3119-C was on fire, so she led a conga line into and then out of the building to pick up SCP-3119. I'll clarify to "into a burning building to retrieve SCP-3119".

Thanks again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone in the description could be tightened up, no doubts there, but I like this enough as-is to upvote in good conscience.

+1

Also tale idea, free to a good home: SCP-3119 x SCP-2331
Don't think I'm the right person to write it, but I'd love to read it. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

I found the description to be confusing, clunky, and somewhat unsubstantiated. It feels like the whole thing was really a set up for the Conga event, though I will admit the mental image there was VERY amusing!

+ Show
- Hide

You're not really being scientific or clinical with this document. For instance, how, exactly, do we know that these things are sapient? How do we even know that they are entities if there's no way to see/measure them? It sounds like you're taking the word of the people "possessed," in which case it could very well be memetic.

We only know of two instances, but you claim that during a "New Yorker" event, they will split in two. So I guess this only happened once, and we're assuming it happens every time? Then you claim the instances "accelerate" away from -C. Like how do we know where they're going, what they're doing if we can't see or track them? How do we even know where they went, or what they did?

SCP-3119 retains its 'Safe' classification because the rate of this exponential growth is slow, and the current number of instances is easy to contain.

Yeah, no, this really needs to be at least Euclid. Things far easier to contain are Euclid.

I want to put down in writing Hannah's dedication to the Foundation during the fire two months ago. In taking that conga line into a burning building she showed both leadership and guts. — Researcher M. Dantzler

What does that even mean? It sounds like you're trying to mix amusing/solemn here, but to me it's just confusing. Why did Hannah Conga into a burning building? Was she trying to neutralize the anomaly? SCP doesn't do that. Why did everyone ELSE follow her? Maybe you meant to say that she Conga'd OUT of the burning building?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I really like this. It's everything that lolFoundation failed at, I think. It's slightly unclinical, but not in a way that breaks suspension of disbelief, and maintains professionalism throughout. It flows very well, from the Foundation distributing amnestics through drugs, to the SCP-containing dance classes, tying everything together delightfully. It's funny, it's well written, and it gets a hard upvote from me.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP! I've loved the site for a long time, and really enjoyed watching it grow, especially with the rise of the Antimemetics division.

My headcanon Foundation is *big* and *old*. Anything with so many sites must have one heck of an HR department. My favourite classification is 'Safe', and so I knew I wanted to try writing something that is stored in Site-19 but could not be put in a box somewhere.
